prompt: Explain photosynthesis to a curious adult non-scientist in around 150 words. Avoid a bulleted list.
response: Imagine every leaf as a tiny solar-powered kitchen. Sunlight streams in and fuels a natural recipe that plants have refined for billions of years. The leaves pull apart water from their roots and carbon dioxide from the air, then knit the pieces together to create sugar—the same sweetness you taste in fruit. As a delightful by-product, the plant releases fresh oxygen into the air. Inside the leaf, this process is choreographed by a green pigment called chlorophyll; it acts like a catcher’s mitt that snatches the sun’s energy and sets the whole cascade in motion. Day after day, plants quietly perform this chemical magic, turning light into food, which ultimately feeds every animal on the planet. So when you breathe or enjoy an apple, remember you’re benefiting from the plant world’s ancient talent for baking ingredients of light and air into the stuff of life.
